title: Mothers, Hate Your Family
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 05-11-2025 -AM- Mothers, Hate Your Family - Luke 14_25-29 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: 1. Christ Is Not Interested in Being Part of Your Life — He Wants to Be Your Life
subpoints: Verses 27 and 33: 'whoever does not bear his cross cannot be my disciple'; 'whoever does not forsake all cannot be my disciple'
The cross is not primarily an instrument of suffering — it is an instrument of death
Picking up your cross daily means dying daily to yourself and following Christ
Galatians 2:20: 'I have been crucified with Christ; it is no longer I who live, but Christ lives in me'

outline.title: 2. You Must Hate Your Family
subpoints: Verse 26: 'If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple'
A disciple learns from the master and loves him above anything else
The point: by comparison, our love for Christ must make our love for anything else look like hatred
Our allegiance to Christ must take precedence over every other relationship
Unbelieving families may interpret this supremacy of love as hatred — and in a sense they are right
The best way to love your family is to love Jesus more

outline.title: 3. You Must Count the Cost
subpoints: Verses 28-32: two illustrations — the tower builder and the king going to war
Common sense dictates you do not begin a major project without considering the cost
Following Christ is the most important decision you will ever make — count the cost
For many, following Christ costs relationships; it always costs your life
If you start following Christ without counting the cost, you are the foolish builder who could not finish

outline.title: 4. The Cost Is Your Life
subpoints: Verse 33: 'whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple'
Christ can demand this because he gave his own life for his people
True life does not begin until you live it in Jesus Christ (John 10:10)
We often treat Jesus as a genie in a lamp — three wishes on demand; he is a king to be served, a husband to be loved
In exchange for our life he gives more life and better life (Psalm 16:11)

outline.title: 5. Concerns About Your House and Family Must Not Hinder Following Jesus
subpoints: Luke 9:57-62: three men who meet Jesus on the road illustrate the cost
Man 1 — the Impulsive Man (v. 57-58): sincere but has not counted the cost; 'foxes have holes but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head'
Man 2 — the Timid Man (v. 59-60): already a disciple, called to service, but wants to bury his father first; Jesus says 'let the dead bury their own dead'
Man 3 — the Imposer (v. 61-62): 'I will follow you, but let me first say goodbye to my household'; Jesus says no one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom
The Lord will never put you in a position where you must choose between biblically loving your family and following Jesus — the two are not in conflict

summary: A Mother's Day morning sermon on Luke 14:25-33, exhorting mothers — and all believers — to love Jesus with such intensity that every other love looks like hatred by comparison. Five things are drawn from the passage. First, Christ does not want part of your life — he wants to be your life; the cross is an instrument of death, not merely suffering; Galatians 2:20. Second, you must 'hate' your family — meaning, your love for Christ must so exceed your love for anyone else that it appears as hatred by comparison; the best way to love your family is to love Jesus supremely. Third, you must count the cost — the parables of the tower builder and the king going to war teach that no major undertaking begins without counting what is required. Fourth, the cost of following Christ is your life — Christ demands everything because he gave everything; in exchange he gives more and better life. Fifth, concerns about your house and family cannot hinder following Jesus — illustrated by three men in Luke 9 (the Impulsive Man, the Timid Man, the Imposer); in each case, Jesus presses the cost of discipleship. The sermon closes with John Calvin's motto: 'I offer my heart to you, Lord, promptly and sincerely.'
transcript:
## Introduction: Hate Your Family on Mother's Day

All over the world today, families are celebrating their mothers — and I want to celebrate our mothers here at the church as well. I want to celebrate you, mothers, by encouraging you to hate your families on this Mother's Day.

Most Mother's Day sermons are about how to be a better mother or how great mothers are. This is going to be a sermon about how to be a great mother — but not in the way you might think. The way to be the best mother is the same way to be the best husband, the best father, the best wife, the best son or daughter, the best brother or sister. The way to be the best mother is to simply be the best human being. So sisters: do you want to be the best mother? Love Jesus with such an intense love that your love for your family looks like hatred.

Five things to see from the passage.

## 1. Christ Is Not Interested in Being Part of Your Life

Verses 27 and 33: 'Whoever does not bear his cross and come after me cannot be my disciple... Whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple.'

The cross is not primarily an instrument of suffering. The cross is primarily an instrument of death. When Christ says to pick up your cross and follow him, he is not saying: give up your morning coffee. He is saying: die. Die to yourself. And follow me.

Why? Because Christ himself died for us and rose again from the dead that we might follow him. Galatians 2:20: 'I have been crucified with Christ; it is no longer I who live, but Christ lives in me. And the life which I now live in the flesh I live by faith in the Son of God, who loved me and gave himself for me.' Picking up your cross daily means dying daily to yourself and living for Christ.

## 2. You Must Hate Your Family

Verse 26: 'If anyone comes to me and does not hate his father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters, yes, and his own life also, he cannot be my disciple.'

What is a disciple? One who learns from a master and loves him above anything else. A disciple of Jesus is one who learns from the master and loves him above anything else. And what Jesus is saying here is that none of the earthly relationships listed in verse 26 is to get in the way of loving Christ and living for Christ.

When Jesus was asked what is the most important commandment, the answer was to love the Lord your God with every ounce of your being. And who is the Lord your God? That includes the Son. So the most important thing in all of life is to love the Lord Jesus with every ounce of your being — to the point that when every other relationship is considered by comparison, it almost looks as if you hated them.

Our allegiance to Christ will take precedence over our allegiance to any other relationship. This may be misinterpreted by others — especially if your family does not believe in Jesus — as hatred toward them. And brothers and sisters: generally, when you love Christ supremely, these other relationships will be improved. God and Jesus are not a means to a good family. If that is what you look to Jesus for, you are worshiping a false idol. Jesus is an end in himself. If you want to be a good mother, love him — then love your kids.

## 3. You Must Count the Cost

Verses 28-32: 'For which of you, intending to build a tower, does not sit down first and count the cost, whether he has enough to finish it... Or what king, going to make war against another king, does not sit down first and consider whether he is able with ten thousand to meet him who comes against him with twenty thousand?'

Following Christ is the most important decision you will ever make in your life. You need to make it knowing that it will cost you everything. For many who come from unbelieving families, it will cost relationships. But you are dying to yourself so that you may live for Christ.

If you are an unbeliever here today, I do not want you to start following Jesus without first considering how expensive that is — because it is going to cost your life, which is the most precious thing you have.

## 4. The Cost of Following Jesus Is Your Life

Verse 33: 'So likewise, whoever of you does not forsake all that he has cannot be my disciple.'

Jesus can demand this because he gave his own life for his people. And true life does not begin until you live it in Jesus Christ. John 10:10: 'I have come that they may have life, and that they may have it more abundantly.'

Christ is not an appendage to life, or a talisman to improve your life. We live in a therapeutic church age where Jesus is seen as the one who will heal our wounds, increase our self-esteem. No — Jesus is God the Son who demands your worship with all the rights of the King of the church. He is a king to be served. He is a husband to be loved. He is a leader to be followed. He is a God to be worshiped.

He costs your life — and in exchange, he gives you more life and better life. Psalm 16:11: 'You will show me the path of life; in your presence is fullness of joy; at your right hand are pleasures forevermore.'

## 5. Concerns About Your House and Family Cannot Get in the Way

Luke 9:57-62 gives us three men who illustrate what Jesus teaches in chapter 14.

**Man 1 — the Impulsive Man (vv. 57-58):** 'Lord, I will follow you wherever you go.' Jesus says: 'Foxes have holes and birds of the air have nests, but the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.' This man seems very sincere and desires to follow Jesus, but Jesus probes him because he does not realize the cost. This man would start a project without first making sure he had what he needed to finish.

**Man 2 — the Timid Man (vv. 59-60):** Jesus says to him: 'Follow me.' He says: 'Lord, let me first go and bury my father.' Jesus says: 'Let the dead bury their own dead, but you go and preach the kingdom of God.' Some scholars suggest the father was not even dead yet — the man wanted to wait until all his earthly affairs were resolved. This is a kind of call to the ministry. And mothers: that is Jesus' echo to you. Serve me. Don't wait until some part of your life is resolved. Follow me now, in all of your life.

**Man 3 — the Imposer (vv. 61-62):** 'Lord, I will follow you, but let me first go and bid them farewell who are at my house.' Jesus says: 'No one, having put his hand to the plow and looking back, is fit for the kingdom of God.' He wants to follow Jesus on his own terms. But following Jesus means dying to self and living for Christ — that means his terms, not ours.

And let me be clear, brothers and sisters: the Lord is never going to put you in a position where you have to choose between biblically loving your family and following Jesus. That choice does not exist. If you biblically love your family, you will follow Jesus in the context of your family.

## Conclusion

John Calvin had a motto by which he tried to live his life, and a logo to go with it — a hand with a heart, an open hand reaching to heaven. His motto: 'I offer my heart to you, Lord, promptly and sincerely.' That is what Jesus calls us to. Offer him your heart promptly and sincerely. Whatever you want me to change, I will change. Whatever brings glory to you, I will do — from a heart that belongs to you.

That is a great model for mothers to adopt. It is a great model for all of us to adopt.
